event: Mary with child holding a red fruit
symbols: red fruit (apple) in the child's hand — reversal of the Fall: the new Adam holds the forbidden fruit without sin
Mary's closeness to the child — the intimacy of the Incarnation, mother and divine Son
white headcovering — Mary's purity and humility
child's sleepy expression — the vulnerability of the Incarnate God
description: A close-up half-length painting of the Virgin Mary holding the Christ Child cheek to cheek. Mary wears a white headscarf wrapped around golden-brown curling hair, and a dark garment with hints of red beneath. The infant Christ, bare-chested with fair reddish hair, holds a small red fruit (apple) in one hand while his other hand grasps Mary's garment. Both figures have soft, delicate features. The child appears drowsy, with half-closed eyes. A Latin inscription runs across the top of the panel against an olive-green background.
theological_description: This tender image of the Virgin and Child with a red apple is laden with theological significance. The apple — the fruit traditionally associated with the Fall of Man — is held not by Eve but by the Christ Child, the new Adam who has come to undo the curse of Eden. Where Eve took the fruit in disobedience leading to death, Christ takes it in his hand as the one who will conquer death through obedience (Rom 5:19). Mary, as the new Eve, holds the child who holds the fruit, signifying that through her 'yes' at the Annunciation (Luke 1:38), the cycle of sin initiated by the first woman is reversed. The cheek-to-cheek embrace echoes the Eleousa (Tenderness) icon tradition, expressing the deep bond between mother and child that is simultaneously human affection and cosmic mystery. The child's drowsy expression may prefigure the sleep of death on the cross — the moment when this innocent flesh will be surrendered for the life of the world.

scripture_refs.reason: Through one man's obedience many will be made righteous — Christ as new Adam holding the fruit of Eden without sin
Eve taking the forbidden fruit — reversed by the Christ Child holding the apple in innocence
Mary's fiat — the new Eve's obedience that reverses the first Eve's disobedience
The last Adam became a life-giving spirit — Christ holding the fruit of death transformed into life
